content: A closure is the combination of a function and the lexical environment in which it was created. In practice: every function in JavaScript
the variables from its surrounding scope, even after that scope has finished executing. This is not a special feature you enable — it is how JavaScript functions work by default.

content.text: Closures are the mechanism behind module patterns, factory functions, memoization, event handlers, and React/Angular hooks. Understanding them deeply removes the mystery from a large class of JavaScript patterns.

attrs.alt: Closure diagrams: scope chain, independent closure instances, loop gotcha, memory retention

content: When a function is created, it receives a hidden reference to its enclosing scope — the
slot in the spec. When the function executes, it looks up variables first in its own scope, then walks up through this chain.
Each call to
creates a
scope with its own
binding. The returned functions close over different scopes — they do not share state.

content.text: The most practically useful closure pattern: expose a limited interface to mutable state while hiding the underlying variables. The variables are truly private — no property access, no prototype traversal can reach them.
Private State vs Class Private Fields

content: TypeScript classes have private fields (
or
). Both approaches achieve encapsulation, but closures are stronger:

content.text: Yes — not a property at all
TypeScript private: not accessible in subclass

content: One of the most-encountered closure bugs: using
in a loop creates a single shared variable for all iterations, so all callbacks close over the same final value.
This is one of the main reasons
was replaced by
and
in ES2015. Always use
in loops.

content.text: Before ES modules, the module pattern used closures to create private scope and a public API. You still encounter it in legacy code and it informs how ES modules work.
Memoization caches a function's return value keyed on its arguments. The cache is private via closure.

A closure holds references to everything in its enclosing scope. If a callback or subscription is never cleaned up, it keeps the entire closure scope alive — including component instances, DOM nodes, and service references.

content: The
operator (Angular 16+) is the idiomatic way to tie an Observable subscription to a component's lifetime. It replaces the older
pattern.

content.text: Two closures created from the same function call share the same scope — mutation through one is visible in the other.
Classic bug: all callbacks close over the same variable. Use
setInterval, addEventListener, and Observable subscriptions all capture component state. Always clean up in
If a closure captures a large array or DOM tree, that memory cannot be freed until the closure itself is collected. Extract only what you need before passing the closure.
If a variable's value looks 'stale' in a callback, the callback may be closing over a variable that was reassigned. Use
Closures naturally appear in test setup and spying. Understanding them prevents subtle test isolation bugs.
re-runs the closure factory, giving each test a private
array. Without re-creating it, tests would share state and ordering would matter.
Every function object created at runtime carries a reference to its outer scope. Creating many closures in hot paths can impact memory and GC.
When closures are cheap vs. expensive
Normal Angular pattern; destroyed with component
Closure created per change detection cycle
Move outside loop; cache with useMemo-style signal
Closure created per list item in *ngFor
Use trackBy and avoid closures in row template
Angular signals memoise the derived value. The filter closure runs only when
changes, not on every template evaluation.

content: A closure is a function that retains access to its creation-time scope. Every JavaScript function is a closure. They enable private state (variables not on any object), factory functions (each call gets independent state), memoization, and the module pattern. The classic pitfall is using
in loops — use

instead. In Angular, closures power computed signals, RxJS operator chains, injection token factories, and event handlers — but they also cause memory leaks when timers, event listeners, or subscriptions are not cleaned up. Use
or the
pipe to tie subscriptions to component lifetime automatically.
